section.title: The Transfiguration and True Greatness
section.sectionRationale: On the way to the cross Jesus is unveiled in glory on the mountain, and the Father's voice commands, Listen to Him. Coming down, He casts out a spirit His disciples could not, drawing out a father's honest cry of faith. He predicts His death a second time, and then repeatedly overturns the disciples' hunger for greatness: true greatness is servanthood, the kingdom belongs to those who welcome children, and sin must be dealt with ruthlessly.
section.thesis: The suffering Son of Man is the beloved Son in glory whom the Father commands us to heed, and those who follow Him must trade rivalry for servanthood, receive the lowly, guard the little ones, and cut off whatever leads to sin, living at peace as salt in the world.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the transfiguration and the Father's voice; the question about Elijah; the boy with an unclean spirit; the second Passion prediction; true greatness and welcoming children; and warnings about causing stumbling, radical dealing with sin, and being salt.

units.label: The transfiguration
units.note: This is My beloved Son, listen to Him

verses.text: After six days Jesus took with Him Peter, James, and John, and led them up a high mountain by themselves. There He was {{metamorphoo|transfigured}} before them.
His clothes became radiantly white, brighter than any launderer on earth could bleach them.
And Elijah and Moses appeared before them, talking with Jesus.
Peter said to Jesus, “Rabbi, it is good for us to be here. Let us put up three shelters, one for You, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.”
For they were all so terrified that Peter did not know what else to say.
Then a cloud appeared and enveloped them, and a voice came from the cloud: “This is My beloved Son. Listen to Him!”
Suddenly, when they looked around, they saw no one with them except Jesus.
As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus admonished them not to tell anyone what they had seen until the Son of Man had risen from the dead.
So they kept this matter to themselves, discussing what it meant to rise from the dead.
And they asked Jesus, “Why do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?”
He replied, “Elijah does indeed come first, and he restores all things. Why then is it written that the Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected?
But I tell you that Elijah has indeed come, and they have done to him whatever they wished, just as it is written about him.”

units.label: The boy with an unclean spirit
units.note: I do believe; help my unbelief

verses.text: When they returned to the other disciples, they saw a large crowd around them, and scribes arguing with them.
As soon as all the people saw Jesus, they were filled with awe and ran to greet Him.
“What are you disputing with them?” He asked.
Someone in the crowd replied, “Teacher, I brought You my son, who has a spirit that makes him mute.
Whenever it seizes him, it throws him to the ground. He foams at the mouth, gnashes his teeth, and becomes rigid. I asked Your disciples to drive it out, but they were unable.”
“O unbelieving generation!” Jesus replied. “How long must I remain with you? How long must I put up with you? Bring the boy to Me.”
So they brought him, and seeing Jesus, the spirit immediately threw the boy into a convulsion. He fell to the ground and rolled around, foaming at the mouth.
Jesus asked the boy’s father, “How long has this been with him?” “From childhood,” he said.
“It often throws him into the fire or into the water, trying to kill him. But if You can do anything, have compassion on us and help us.”
“If You can?” echoed Jesus. “All things are possible to him who believes!”
Immediately the boy’s father cried out, “I do believe; help my unbelief!”
When Jesus saw that a crowd had come running, He rebuked the unclean spirit. “You deaf and mute spirit,” He said, “I command you to come out and never enter him again.”
After shrieking and convulsing him violently, the spirit came out. The boy became like a corpse, so that many said, “He is dead.”
But Jesus took him by the hand and helped him to his feet, and he stood up.
After Jesus had gone into the house, His disciples asked Him privately, “Why couldn’t we drive it out?”
Jesus answered, “This kind cannot come out, except by prayer.”

units.label: The second Passion prediction
units.note: delivered into the hands of men

verses.text: Going on from there, they passed through Galilee. But Jesus did not want anyone to know,
because He was teaching His disciples. He told them, “The Son of Man will be delivered into the hands of men. They will kill Him, and after three days He will rise.”
But they did not understand this statement, and they were afraid to ask Him about it.

units.label: True greatness and welcoming children
units.note: the last of all and the servant of all

verses.text: Then they came to Capernaum. While Jesus was in the house, He asked them, “What were you discussing on the way?”
But they were silent, for on the way they had been arguing about which of them was the greatest.
Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said, “If anyone wants to be first, he must be the last of all and the {{diakonos|servant}} of all.”
Then He had a little child stand among them. Taking the child in His arms, He said to them,
“Whoever welcomes one of these little children in My name welcomes Me, and whoever welcomes Me welcomes not only Me, but the One who sent Me.”
John said to Him, “Teacher, we saw someone else driving out demons in Your name, and we tried to stop him, because he does not accompany us.”
“Do not stop him,” Jesus replied. “For no one who performs a miracle in My name can turn around and speak evil of Me.
For whoever is not against us is for us.
Indeed, if anyone gives you even a cup of water because you bear the name of Christ, truly I tell you, he will never lose his reward.
But if anyone causes one of these little ones who believe in Me to {{skandalizo|stumble}}, it would be better for him to have a large millstone hung around his neck and to be thrown into the sea.
If your hand causes you to sin, cut it off. It is better for you to enter life crippled than to have two hands and go into {{gehenna|hell}}, into the unquenchable fire.
If your foot causes you to sin, cut it off. It is better for you to enter life lame than to have two feet and be thrown into hell.
And if your eye causes you to sin, pluck it out. It is better for you to enter the kingdom of God with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into hell,
where ‘their worm never dies, and the fire is never quenched.’
For everyone will be salted with fire.
{{halas|Salt}} is good, but if the salt loses its saltiness, with what will you season it? Have salt among yourselves, and be at peace with one another.”

metamorphoo.gloss: To transform, to change form. On the mountain Jesus is transfigured, His inner glory shining through His body and clothes. It is a glimpse of who He truly is and of the glory that lies beyond the cross, confirming that the suffering Son is the glorious Son of God.

diakonos.gloss: A servant, one who waits on others. Jesus overturns the world's ladder of greatness: to be first in the kingdom is to be last and the servant of all. Greatness is measured by lowly service, modelled by the Son of Man who came to serve and give His life.

skandalizo.gloss: To cause to stumble, to trip into sin. Jesus warns with terrible seriousness against causing one of His little ones to stumble, and against whatever in ourselves leads us into sin; the danger of sin is grave enough to warrant the most drastic response.

gehenna.gloss: Hell, from the Valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, a place of burning and refuse associated with judgment. Jesus uses it for the final judgment of the unrepentant, the unquenchable fire, underscoring how seriously sin must be taken and how real the stakes are.

halas.gloss: Salt, used to preserve, purify, and flavour, and a token of covenant. Disciples are to have salt in themselves, a purifying, preserving character tested by fire, and to keep peace with one another rather than the rivalry that had just consumed them.

basic.context: Six days after foretelling His death, Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a mountain and is transfigured before them, blazing with glory, with Moses and Elijah beside Him, until the Father's voice says, This is My beloved Son, listen to Him. Coming down, Jesus casts out a spirit the disciples could not, and the boy's father cries, I believe, help my unbelief. Jesus predicts His death again, but the disciples, instead of grasping it, argue about who is the greatest. Jesus answers with a series of reversals: to be first is to be servant of all, to welcome a child is to welcome Him, no one who works in His name is against Him, causing a little one to stumble is dreadful, and sin must be cut out ruthlessly. He ends by calling them to be salt and to be at peace with one another.
basic.hermeneutics: The glory of the transfiguration confirms the message of the cross; the Father's command, listen to Him, endorses the suffering path Jesus just announced. The rest of the chapter shows how badly the disciples misunderstand: while Jesus speaks of dying, they compete for status. Jesus repeatedly inverts human values, defining greatness as service and warning that the stakes of sin are eternal.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is metamorphoo, transfigured, in glory. He teaches that the great must be diakonos, servant, of all. He warns against causing little ones to skandalizo, stumble, and against sin that leads to gehenna, hell. And He calls disciples to be halas, salt, at peace with one another.
basic.typology: Moses and Elijah, the law and the prophets, appear with Jesus and then fade, leaving Him alone: He is the one they pointed to. The transfiguration on the mountain echoes God's glory on Sinai. John the Baptist is the promised Elijah who suffers before the Son of Man suffers.
basic.christ: Jesus is the beloved Son, radiant in divine glory, to whom the Father says listen. He is the servant-King who defines greatness by lowliness, and the Son of Man who will be delivered up, killed, and rise.
basic.god: The Father openly declares and commands allegiance to His Son. God's values overturn the world's: the last is first, the servant is great, the child is welcomed, and sin is deadly serious.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 24 and 34 describe glory on the mountain. Malachi 4 promises Elijah. Isaiah 66 supplies the undying worm and unquenchable fire.
basic.oneStory: On the way to the cross, heaven pulls back the curtain: the suffering Son is the glorious Son of God, and the Father commands us to heed Him. His followers, still grasping for greatness, must learn the upside-down way of the kingdom, service, humility, and ruthless holiness, the way of the One who came not to be served but to serve, and to give His life.

context.literary: The transfiguration answers Jesus' promise in 9:1 that some would see the kingdom come with power, and confirms the confession and Passion prediction just before. The rest of chapter 9 forms part of the journey to Jerusalem, structured around Jesus' Passion predictions, each followed by the disciples' failure to understand and by teaching on discipleship. Here the second prediction is met by an argument about greatness.
context.historical: The high mountain is traditionally Tabor or, more likely given the setting, a spur of Hermon near Caesarea Philippi. Rabbinic and popular expectation held that Elijah would come before the Messiah, based on Malachi. Gehenna, the Valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem, had been a site of child sacrifice and later a burning rubbish dump, a vivid image of judgment.
context.canonical: The shining glory and the cloud recall the Sinai theophany, Exodus 24:15-18, and the Father's words echo Psalm 2:7 and Deuteronomy 18:15, listen to Him, marking Jesus as the prophet greater than Moses. Malachi 4:5-6 promises Elijah, fulfilled in John. The undying worm and unquenchable fire come from Isaiah 66:24. Jesus' teaching on servanthood is embodied at the cross, Mark 10:45.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confirm, by the glory of the transfiguration and the Father's command, that the suffering Son of Man is the beloved Son to be obeyed, and to reshape the disciples' distorted values by teaching servanthood, the welcome of the lowly, and the deadly seriousness of sin, all on the road to the cross.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The transfiguration and exorcism describe unique revelatory events. The teaching on greatness, welcoming children, guarding little ones, and dealing radically with sin is prescriptive for all disciples. The vivid commands to cut off hand or foot are deliberate hyperbole, urging decisive action against sin, not literal self-mutilation.

debates.question: What does the father's cry, I believe, help my unbelief, teach about faith?

views.label: Faith and struggle can coexist
views.case: The father genuinely believes yet is honest about his weakness; Jesus honours his imperfect, growing faith, showing that faith need not be flawless to lay hold of Christ, only real and directed to Him.

views.label: Faith itself is a gift to be sought from Jesus
views.case: The man asks Jesus to help even his unbelief, acknowledging that the power and the faith alike come from Christ; genuine faith casts itself on Jesus not only for the miracle but for faith to believe.

debates.bearing: Both readings comfort weak believers: Jesus welcomes honest, struggling faith and is the one to whom we bring even our doubt, asking Him to strengthen it.

debates.question: Does Jesus command literal self-mutilation to avoid sin?

views.label: Vivid hyperbole for decisive action
views.case: Jesus uses shocking overstatement to convey that nothing, however precious, is worth clinging to if it drags us into sin; the point is ruthless, wholehearted repentance, not literal amputation, which would not cure a sinful heart.

views.label: A sober measure of eternal stakes
views.case: The extremity of the language matches the extremity of the danger, hell's unquenchable fire; better to lose anything now than to be lost forever, so the hyperbole presses the infinite worth of the soul.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the commands dramatize the seriousness of sin and the value of eternal life, calling for radical, inward dealing with sin rather than physical self-harm.

study.originalLanguages: On the mountain Jesus is metamorphoo, transfigured, His hidden glory breaking through, and the Father commands the disciples to listen to His beloved Son. Coming down into a world of struggling faith and stubborn evil, Jesus casts out the spirit and then reorders His followers' values: to be first is to be diakonos, the servant of all, and to welcome a child is to welcome Him. He warns with severe language against causing His little ones to skandalizo, stumble, and against whatever in us leads to sin, for the alternative is gehenna, hell, the unquenchable fire. Finally He calls them to be halas, salt, purifying and preserving, tested by fire, keeping peace with one another instead of jockeying for greatness.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus as the greater Moses, radiant with divine glory, the Sinai theophany surpassed in the beloved Son, Exodus 34:29-35 and 2 Corinthians 3:18.

typology.type: Moses and Elijah yielding to Jesus
typology.fulfillment: The law and the prophets fulfilled in Christ, who alone remains when they fade, Luke 24:27 and Hebrews 1:1-2.

typology.type: Elijah who comes first and suffers
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist as the promised Elijah, mistreated before the Son of Man is rejected, Malachi 4:5 and Mark 6:14-29.

study.christ: The transfiguration unveils Jesus as the beloved Son, ablaze with the glory that is His by right, flanked by Moses and Elijah who then vanish, leaving Him alone as the one the law and prophets foretold. The Father's command, listen to Him, sets divine authority behind the message of the cross Jesus has just begun to teach. Yet the same glorious Son stoops into the mess of the world below to heal a tormented boy, to bear with slow disciples, and to walk deliberately toward being delivered into the hands of men. He is the servant-King who defines greatness by lowly service, embodying in Himself the upside-down kingdom He proclaims, the One who will be least of all by dying for all.
study.god: God the Father openly identifies Jesus as His beloved Son and commands that He be heard, endorsing the way of suffering the disciples found so hard. God's kingdom runs on values that reverse the world's: the first is last, the great is the servant, the child is embraced, the outsider working in Christ's name is welcomed, and the smallest kindness in His name is remembered. And God takes sin with utter seriousness; the warnings of unquenchable fire reveal a holy God before whom the stakes are eternal, so that nothing is too costly to give up in order to enter life.

crossReferences.note: Moses' face shining after meeting God on the mountain, the glory the transfigured Christ surpasses from within Himself.
God will raise up a prophet like Moses; listen to Him, echoed in the Father's command at the transfiguration.
The promise to send Elijah before the great day, fulfilled in John the Baptist.
The great must be servant, for the Son of Man came to serve and give His life, the pattern of true greatness taught here.

study.oneStory: On the road to the cross, heaven draws back the curtain: the suffering Son of Man is the beloved Son of God, radiant in glory, and the Father commands the world to heed Him. The law and the prophets, Moses and Elijah, step aside for Him. Yet the disciples still crave greatness while Jesus speaks of dying, so He patiently teaches the upside-down way of the kingdom, service, humility, welcome of the lowly, and ruthless holiness. It is the way of the One who, though clothed in glory, will make Himself least of all, delivered into the hands of men, to serve and to save. The story presses on toward Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What did the transfiguration reveal, and what did the Father command?

options.text: Jesus' hidden divine glory as the beloved Son, and the Father's command to listen to Him.
options.note: Mark 9:2-7; the glory confirms His identity and the voice commands obedience to Him.

options.text: That Jesus was a great prophet alongside Moses and Elijah.
options.note: Moses and Elijah fade and Jesus alone remains; He is far more than a prophet beside them.

options.text: Jesus shining in glory, confirming the message of the cross He had just taught.
options.note: True and important, but it should name the Father's command, listen to Him, and Jesus as beloved Son.

options.text: That the disciples should build three shelters to stay on the mountain.
options.note: Peter's suggestion was mistaken; the point was the glory of the Son and the Father's command.

quiz.q: What does the father's cry, I do believe, help my unbelief, show?

options.text: That real but struggling faith can lay hold of Jesus, who welcomes honest, imperfect trust.
options.note: Mark 9:24; Jesus honours the man's genuine yet weak faith and heals his son.

options.text: That the man had no faith at all.
options.note: He says I do believe; his faith was real though mixed with weakness.

options.text: That faith and doubt can exist together in a sincere heart seeking Jesus.
options.note: True, but the fuller point is that Jesus welcomes and strengthens such honest, imperfect faith.

options.text: That faith must be perfect before Jesus will act.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus acts for a man whose faith is admittedly imperfect.

quiz.q: How does Jesus define true greatness?

options.text: To be first, one must be the last of all and the servant of all.
options.note: Mark 9:35; greatness in the kingdom is lowly service.

options.text: By welcoming children and the lowly in Jesus' name.
options.note: A true expression of it, but the core statement is that the first must be servant of all.

options.text: By achieving the most impressive works for God.
options.note: Jesus measures greatness by humble service, not by impressive achievement.

options.text: By ruling over others as the world's leaders do.
options.note: Jesus overturns that model; the great must serve, not lord it over others.

quiz.q: What is the point of Jesus' shocking words about cutting off a hand or plucking out an eye?

options.text: Vivid hyperbole urging radical, decisive dealing with sin, since eternal life is worth losing anything to gain.
options.note: The extreme images press the seriousness of sin and the worth of entering life, not literal mutilation.

options.text: A literal command to remove body parts that sin.
options.note: It is deliberate overstatement; mutilation could not cure a sinful heart.

options.text: A call to take sin with utmost seriousness because the stakes are eternal.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should recognize the language as hyperbole urging decisive action.

options.text: A suggestion that sin is not really that dangerous.
options.note: The opposite; the extremity of the language shows how gravely dangerous sin is.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by calling His disciples to have salt among themselves?

options.text: To have a purifying, preserving character and to be at peace with one another rather than rivals for greatness.
options.note: Mark 9:50 ties salt to peace, answering their argument about who was greatest.

options.text: To add flavour to their conversations.
options.note: Salt here signifies purifying character and peace, not merely lively talk.

options.text: To live distinct, godly lives that influence the world around them.
options.note: A true dimension of being salt, but here Jesus specifically links it to peace among themselves.

options.text: To perform religious rituals with salt.
options.note: Jesus speaks of character and peace, not ritual.

questions: The Father said of Jesus, listen to Him. In what area of your life is Jesus asking to be heard and obeyed rather than merely admired?
The father prayed, I believe, help my unbelief. How might that honest prayer give you courage to bring your own struggling faith to Jesus?
The disciples argued about greatness while Jesus spoke of dying. Where do you catch yourself competing for status instead of seeking to serve?
Jesus said welcoming a lowly child is welcoming Him. Who are the overlooked or unimportant people God is calling you to receive in His name?
Jesus urged radical action against whatever leads to sin. What specific thing might you need to cut off decisively for the sake of your walk with God?

translationNotes.note: This kind cannot come out except by prayer; some manuscripts add and fasting. Either way Jesus locates the disciples' failure in prayerless self-reliance rather than a wrong technique.

The BSB does not print verses 44 and 46, since the words repeated there, their worm never dies and the fire is never quenched, are not in the earliest manuscripts and are already given in verse 48; the numbering therefore skips 44 and 46.
Their worm never dies, and the fire is never quenched quotes Isaiah 66:24, a picture of final judgment that Jesus applies to gehenna, hell.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 9:2-50. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. Verses 44 and 46 are absent from the BSB main text and are noted in translationNotes; verses run 2 to 43, then 45, 47 to 50. The em dash in verse 5 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
